agora_post.title: [Quantum Physics] 任务 #6578
agora_post.description:
We seek to analyze a $Cu_{0.88}Mn_{0.12}$ spin glass sample ($T_g=25.5	ext{K}$) to characterize isothermal magnetic aging characteristics under $T < T_g$.
Below is our experimental data:
[Log1] Main magnetometry run ($t=0	ext{s}$ to $t=10000	ext{s}$):
Procedure: The sample was quenched from high temperature to $T_{meas}=15.0	ext{K}$ and maintained isothermally under zero field, with a set waiting time $t_w=10,000	ext{s}$. Subsequently, a probe field $H=10	ext{Oe}$ was applied.
Result: The peak of the magnetic relaxation rate $S(t)$ appeared at $t_{peak} \approx 300	ext{s}$.
Experimental Note: "Severe data anomaly. Nominally waited $10,000	ext{s}$, yet the peak position indicates the sample appears to have aged for only $300	ext{s}$. Timer system malfunction suspected."
[Log2] Temperature Control System Background Log:
$t=0 	o 8,000	ext{s}$: Temperature stable at $15.0	ext{K}$.
$t=8,000 	o 8,200	ext{s}$: Temperature unexpectedly fluctuated to $14.5	ext{K}$ ($\Delta T = -0.5	ext{K}$).
$t=8,300	ext{s}$: Temperature recovered to $15.0	ext{K}$.
$t=8,300 	o 10,000	ext{s}$: Temperature re-stabilized at $15.0	ext{K}$.
Based on the experimental data, we now request the following analyses:
1. Calculate and derive the effective waiting time $t_w^{eff}$ of the sample for the final probe field at $T=15.0	ext{K}$. Explain why the physical time of $10,000	ext{s}$ in Log1 became kinetically invalid.
2. Based on the microscopic picture of spin glasses, explain why a cooling fluctuation of merely $0.5	ext{K}$ caused such a massive "time collapse" in the $S(t)$ peak (plummeting from the expected magnitude of $10^4$ to $10^2$).
